region_of_origin: Central Asia (widely cultivated globally; sold at all wet markets and supermarkets in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Allium aromatics, raw — fresh peeled cloves

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw garlic clove weighs approximately 5g and provides about 7 kcal; realistic culinary use is 1–3 cloves (5–15g) per dish.

nutrient_source.title: Garlic, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 169230)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw garlic, FDC ID 169230. All per-100g nutrient values sourced from this entry. Confidence: very high.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Garlic
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~10–15 (very low) for raw garlic per University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 5g serving = 0.3 (negligible). At realistic culinary portions (5–15g), garlic contributes negligible glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Allicin content — antihypertensive effect at therapeutic raw doses
hypertension.guidance: Raw garlic at 3–5 cloves/day (600–900mg allicin equivalent) has demonstrated a modest antihypertensive effect of approximately 4–5 mmHg systolic reduction in RCT meta-analyses; this benefit requires raw garlic consumed consistently for 8+ weeks — cooked garlic destroys allicin and provides no antihypertensive effect. Sodium content is low at 17mg per 100g, posing no concern for hypertension management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~10) and potential modest insulin-sensitising effects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic has a GI of approximately 10 (very low) and at realistic culinary portions of 5–15g contributes a glycemic load of less than 0.5, making it entirely safe for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; allicin derivatives may have modest insulin-sensitising effects (Grade C evidence), though culinary doses do not meaningfully alter postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Antiplatelet activity, lipid-modulating potential, and very low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw garlic contains organosulfur compounds (allicin, ajoene, diallyl disulfide) with mild antiplatelet and lipid-modulating properties supported by meta-analyses; saturated fat is negligible at 0.1g per 100g and cholesterol is 0mg, making garlic a cardiovascular-friendly aromatic; patients on warfarin or antiplatelet therapy should be aware that high raw garlic intake (>5g/day) may potentiate antiplatelet effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: NF-κB inhibition by allicin breakdown products at raw garlic doses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Allicin and its breakdown products (diallyl sulphide, ajoene) have demonstrated NF-κB inhibition in cell studies, suggesting modest anti-inflammatory potential; cooked garlic contains no residual allicin and therefore no anti-inflammatory organosulfur activity, while raw garlic at culinary doses provides modest allicin activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic (Raw) is a pungent allium bulb used universally as a cooking aromatic and flavour base. Per 100g raw peeled cloves (USDA SR Legacy FDC 169230): 149 kcal, 6.4g protein, 0.5g fat, 33.1g carbohydrate, 2.1g dietary fibre, 17mg sodium, 401mg potassium, 181mg calcium, 1.7mg iron, 31.2mg vitamin C, and 1.24mg vitamin B6 (approximately 73% of adult RDA). Glycemic index is approximately 10 (very low); at a realistic culinary portion of 5–15g (1–3 medium cloves), the glycemic load is under 0.5 — entirely negligible. ALLICIN STABILITY: allicin is only produced enzymatically when raw garlic is crushed or chopped; it is completely destroyed by heat above 60°C. Consequently, cooked garlic has zero allicin and zero antihypertensive or antiplatelet organosulfur activity. Antihypertensive benefit (~4–5 mmHg systolic reduction) requires raw garlic at 3–5 cloves/day consistently for 8+ weeks. Antiplatelet effect is mild at >5g/day raw — clinically relevant for patients on warfarin or aspirin. Garlic is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is sold at all wet markets and supermarkets in Singapore. This record is a per-100g raw reference; realistic culinary use is 5–15g per dish (7–22 kcal).
common_user_queries: how many calories in a garlic clove

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169230 (raw garlic), an approved primary source with very high reliability. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified direct matches. No estimation or analogy required.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 169230 — garlic is a plant food and inherently contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). Vitamin A (RAE) is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — garlic contains negligible retinol activity equivalents (confirmed zero). Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — garlic is a plant food and contains no cobalamin (naturally zero). Vitamin D is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — garlic contains no vitamin D (naturally zero). Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy (confirmed zero). Monounsaturated fat (0.0g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.2g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 169230. Water content (58.6g per 100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 169230. ALLICIN NOTE: allicin is only produced when raw garlic is crushed or chopped and is completely destroyed by cooking above 60°C; all antihypertensive and antiplatelet benefits require raw garlic. Realistic culinary portion is 5–15g (1–3 medium cloves) per dish, yielding approximately 7–22 kcal. This record is a generic per-100g raw reference entry; it is not a cooked-garlic record.
